name: CLAUSE — Agentic Neuro-Symbolic Knowledge Graph Reasoning via Dynamic Learnable Context Engineering
maintainer: Yang Zhao (Independent Researcher), Chengxiao Dai (University of Sydney), Wei Zhuo, Yue Xiu, Dusit Niyato (Nanyang Technological University, Singapore)
license: [uncertain] — no GitHub repository or explicit license found; research paper only (arXiv 2509.21035, submitted September 25, 2025, accepted OpenReview)
last_major_release: [uncertain] — research paper only; no versioned software release found. arXiv 2509.21035 submitted September 2025.
activity: [uncertain] — no public GitHub repository confirmed as of 2026-04. Paper indexed on arXiv and OpenReview. No star count, contributor count, or commit history available.
storage_backend: Knowledge Graph (KG) — paper experiments use Wikidata-derived subgraph (HotpotQA), MetaQA movie KG, and DBpedia-grounded FactKG. No specific database engine mandated; KGs are treated as abstract edge-node structures. Implementation backend not specified in paper.
representation_primitive: Entity+edge (knowledge graph triples). The Subgraph Architect constructs a question-anchored subgraph of KG entities and edges. The Path Navigator traverses entity-relation-entity paths. The Context Curator textualizes selected KG snippets (entity-relation-entity triples rendered as natural language). Core primitive is a directed labeled graph: nodes are entities (with type and text labels), edges are named relations.
api_shape: Research prototype only — no Python library, MCP server, REST API, or CLI documented. The framework is described algorithmically (LC-MAPPO training + inference loop). No first-party MCP server. Implementation would require building from the paper's algorithmic specification.
hosting: Self-host (research prototype). No managed cloud service.
llm_dependency: Retrieval phase: LLM used by the Context Curator to textualize KG snippets for the final answer generation step. The three RL-trained agents (Subgraph Architect, Path Navigator, Context Curator) use learned policies (LC-MAPPO), not LLMs, for their core decisions. LLM is required for answer synthesis from the curated context. Models used in experiments: [uncertain] — not specified in accessible search results.
schema_extensibility: Fixed (KG-dependent). The entity and relation types are determined by the underlying knowledge graph (e.g., Wikidata properties, MetaQA movie relations, DBpedia ontology). CLAUSE agents operate on whatever schema the KG exposes. No mechanism for LLM-driven schema discovery or YAML-config extension. Schema extensibility is inherited from the KG source.
schema_richness: Depends entirely on the underlying KG. In experiments: Wikidata (millions of entity types, thousands of relation types), MetaQA (~9 entity types, ~9 relation types in the movie domain), DBpedia (~700 ontology classes, ~1,000 properties). CLAUSE itself imposes no fixed schema. For a Gimle-style deployment with a custom code KG, the schema richness would be defined by the Gimle schema (20+ entities / 15+ edges). Multi-label: supported (KG entities can carry multiple types). Multi-graph: supported (separate KGs per experiment).
temporal_model: None in the core framework. KGs used in experiments (Wikidata, DBpedia) have external temporal properties, but CLAUSE does not expose a bi-temporal or event-log model. Subgraph construction is stateless per query — no session memory or temporal tracking across queries.
forgetting_policy: None — CLAUSE is a stateless per-query retrieval system. No persistent memory between queries; each query starts from a fresh KG traversal. The Context Curator selects a minimal evidence set per query but does not maintain a memory store subject to eviction.
ingest_latency_class: Lazy-on-demand (deferred until query). CLAUSE does not pre-index or pre-ingest; it traverses the KG at query time. The budget-aware agents decide how much to expand the subgraph per query. End-to-end latency: −18.6% vs GraphRAG baseline on MetaQA-2-hop benchmark.
retrieval_modes: Graph-traversal (primary) + context selection. The Subgraph Architect performs question-anchored subgraph expansion (policy-guided). The Path Navigator discovers and revises multi-hop reasoning paths. The Context Curator assembles a minimal textualized snippet set. No vector search or BM25 in the core framework; KG structure is the sole retrieval substrate.
retrieval_agenticity: CLAUSE-budget-aware (defines this category). The three-agent framework treats retrieval as a sequential decision process: Subgraph Architect decides which KG nodes to include (expand/prune), Path Navigator decides which reasoning paths to follow or backtrack, Context Curator decides which evidence snippets to keep and when to stop. All decisions are governed by LC-MAPPO policies optimized under Lagrangian constraints on edge edits, interaction steps, and selected tokens. This is the most sophisticated agentic retrieval pattern in the survey period.
retrieval_budget_control: Yes — first-class. Edge edit budget, interaction step budget, and selected token budget are user-specified as Lagrangian constraints in LC-MAPPO. Per-query adaptation to trade-offs among accuracy, latency, and cost without retraining. Feasibility rate (constraint adherence) at 0.340 with LC-MAPPO vs 0.117 with standard MAPPO — 191% improvement in constraint adherence.
multi_project_namespace: None defined. CLAUSE is a per-query retrieval system over a single KG. Multi-KG support would require separate model instances. No project-label or group_id concept.
multi_user_access: None. Single-agent retrieval system. No RBAC, per-agent namespace, or provenance-aware sharing.
embedder_options: [uncertain] — paper does not specify embedding models. Entity linking and question anchoring likely use standard KG entity embeddings (e.g., TransE, RotatE, or BERT-based entity linkers). No built-in adapter registry described.
llm_client_options: [uncertain] — paper uses an LLM for final answer generation but does not specify which LLM client. The RL-trained agents are separate from the LLM; the LLM is a downstream reader. LLM can theoretically be replaced with any generation model.
cost_privacy: Data stays local (research prototype with local KG). Token cost: the Context Curator minimizes selected tokens as a constrained objective — CLAUSE achieves the smallest edge budgets (0.74–0.90 normalized) versus GraphRAG (1.18–1.55), implying significantly lower token costs at answer time. No cloud service; privacy not a concern for the research artifact.
code_artifact_linkage: None first-party in the general framework. However, CLAUSE's KG-traversal architecture is directly applicable to a code KG: if Gimle's Neo4j graph contains Symbol, File, Module, and PR nodes, CLAUSE-style agents could traverse call-graph edges and import edges to answer multi-hop code questions. The framework is KG-agnostic — linkage granularity is limited only by what the underlying KG exposes. Ranked: none native, but the traversal pattern is transferable to file-path / symbol / line-level-defs+refs chains.
code_analysis_features: None first-party. CLAUSE is a KG reasoning framework, not a code analysis tool. Code analysis features (call graph, diff-awareness, symbol index) would be provided by a code-KG extractor (e.g., Gimle's own extractors), not by CLAUSE itself.
memory_os_layer: Plaintext only (in MemOS taxonomy). CLAUSE operates at the plaintext tier: it retrieves and textualizes KG facts for LLM context injection. No activation (KV-cache) or parametric (weight-level) memory tiers. Cross-type migration: not applicable.
self_improvement_mechanism: RL-policy (LC-MAPPO). The three agents are trained with Lagrangian-constrained MARL to optimize subgraph construction, path navigation, and evidence selection jointly. This is a training-time self-improvement via RL, not an online execution-feedback loop. No ACE-GRC or manual curation; the policy learns from reward signals during offline training.
provenance_standard: Custom-log / implicit. KG triples naturally carry provenance (entity IDs, relation URIs, source graph). The Context Curator selects a minimal evidence set with identifiable KG nodes, providing implicit provenance for answers. No W3C-PROV, OpenTelemetry, or MCP-native-trace documented. The textualized snippets can be traced back to specific KG triples.
benchmark_results: MetaQA-2-hop vs strongest RAG baseline (GraphRAG): +39.3 EM@1, −18.6% latency, −40.9% edge growth. Overall across HotpotQA, MetaQA 1/2/3-hop, and FactKG: CLAUSE achieves best accuracy on all datasets and hops — HotpotQA: 71.7 EM (vs Hybrid RAG 66.0, KG-Agent 68.7); FactKG: 84.2 EM; MetaQA 1/2/3-hop: 91.0 / 87.3 / 85.5 EM. Smallest edge budgets across all settings (0.74–0.90 normalized vs GraphRAG 1.18–1.55). LC-MAPPO feasibility rate 0.340 vs standard MAPPO 0.117 (191% improvement in constraint adherence). Results from the paper's own evaluation; independent third-party replication not confirmed.
strengths: 1. First-class budget-aware traversal: the Lagrangian-constrained RL framework is the only system in the survey that exposes edge/step/token budgets as hard per-query constraints with measurable adherence — directly addresses Gimle's need for predictable cost/latency on large code KGs. 2. Proven multi-hop KG reasoning gains: +39.3 EM@1 over GraphRAG with lower latency on standard benchmarks — the three-agent decomposition (expand, traverse, curate) is a principled architecture for code-graph multi-hop queries (e.g., which PRs transitively affect this function). 3. KG-agnostic architecture: CLAUSE makes no assumptions about the KG schema — it would work on Gimle's Neo4j code graph with Symbol/File/Module nodes as naturally as on Wikidata or DBpedia.
weaknesses: 1. Research prototype without public code: no confirmed GitHub release or production-ready implementation as of 2026-04 — significant engineering effort required to adopt. 2. Stateless per-query, no session memory: CLAUSE has no persistent memory across queries — each retrieval starts from scratch; does not address Gimle's need for session-level context accumulation or bi-temporal tracking. 3. RL training cost: LC-MAPPO requires offline RL training on domain-specific reward signals before deployment — adds upfront cost and domain-specific labeled data or simulator for training, limiting plug-and-play adoption.
fit_with_gimle_section_5: Verdict: steal-specific-ideas — specifically the budget-aware three-agent traversal pattern. Do not adopt whole — no public code, no session memory, no bi-temporal model. Ideas to steal: (A) Three-agent KG retrieval decomposition: when Gimle's retrieval layer needs to answer multi-hop questions over the code KG (e.g., which Decisions influenced the current Module's architecture), decompose retrieval into three sub-agents: Subgraph Architect (anchors to queried entities, expands relevant neighborhood), Path Navigator (follows relationship chains: Symbol to File to Module to Decision), Context Curator (selects minimal evidence set within token budget). Example traversal spec: {anchor: Symbol:process_payment, budget: {max_hops: 3, max_edges: 50, max_tokens: 2048}, relations: [defined_in, calls, part_of, influenced_by]}. (B) Lagrangian budget enforcement as a first-class API parameter: expose max_hops, max_edges, max_tokens as explicit parameters on Gimle's graph retrieval API calls, enabling per-query cost/latency adaptation without model retraining. This is a design pattern, not a library dependency. (C) Minimal evidence curation: the Context Curator's principle of assembling the smallest sufficient evidence set is directly applicable to Gimle's context injection — rather than dumping the full neighborhood, select only the KG triples necessary for the current LLM call. Incompatible aspects: CLAUSE's LC-MAPPO training requirement is impractical for Gimle's current stage; a rule-based or LLM-guided approximation of the three-agent pattern is more appropriate initially.
